Re: Santa Clara County Transportation Agency Newsletter on
"Measure A"
Dear Ms. Turvill:
on behalf of the santa clara county Transit District, 
I
request advice as to whether the attached article on "Measure A, "
which appeared in Transit Newsletters, is affected by any of the
Iaws within your jurisdiction.
In June , Lgg2, this office gave written advice to the
Transportation Agency which described the principles contained in
caseliw (primarity Stanson v. Mott (1976) L7 Ca1.3d 206>, and
concluded:
"Agency staff may not "take sides" in the election
or use public funds or authority to advocate Passage
or defeat of the tax measure. However, Agency staff
may provide information relevant to the tax measure
to anyone as long as the information constitutes a
fair and balanced representation of the facts, and
avoids advocating either the enactment or the defeat
of the tax measure. For example, if any group
advocating or opposing the tax requests staff
attendance at a group meeting, that attendance must
be Iimited to providing information, and staff must
avoid advocating passage or defeat of the measure.
Of course, individual County or District employees
may 
on their own time, dt their own expense, and
acting in their capacity as private citizens and not
under color of County or District authority --
exercise their right to participate in the electoral
process as they see fit." 
(June 29, L992 memorandum
irom william Anderson to Larry Reuter)
Chief Assistant County Counsel: Ann Miller Ravel
Chief Deputies: Robe rt J. Menifee. Susan G. Levenberg. william I. Anderson
O
gs


Lt r . /FPPC
october 6, t992
Page Two
In mid-August, this office reviewed a draft of the "Measure A
Transportation Initiative 
Explained" article prior to its
publication. 
The office concluded that the information describing
the measure, its Purposes anal the membership on the Transportation
Authority, was a neutral and objective description of the measure.
Since Measure A t"rould affect the District and its ridership, we
ailvised orally that it would be permissible to include the Measure
A description in Agency newsletters.
At the time the article was in draft form, the office had
before it the Iast two paragraphs of the article which describe
the proponents of the measure as well as listing their address and
telephone number. Readers are referreal to the proponents "if you
would like more information on Measure A" ' Although this kintl of
referral does not aclvocate a "yes" vote, it can be viewed as
one-sitlecl because it features only the proponents. we were of the
view then, however, that since there was no known organized
opposition to the measure, it would be appropriate to Iist the
name and address of the proPonents. Our view has changeil because
it is now clear that there is opposition. It is therefore no
longer appropriate to continue to disseminate information which
makes no reference to the organized opposition.
I understand that your office does not give advice on matters
covered outsicle the Fair PoIitical Practices Act. However, there
may be issues covered by the Act, such as whether the cost of the
neisletter is a reportable contribution, upon which your office
cloes advise.
very truly yours,
/i.tu-4/vrd'd/
STEVEN M. WOODSIDE
County Counsel

sections.question: On behalf of the Santa Clara County Transit District, I request advice as to whether the attached article on "Measure A," which appeared in Transit Newsletters, is affected by any of the laws within your jurisdiction. Specifically, whether the cost of the newsletter is a reportable contribution and whether the newsletter's content, which lists only proponents of Measure A with their contact information, constitutes permissible information dissemination under the Fair Political Practices Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Does the Transit District's newsletter article on Measure A, which describes the measure neutrally but lists only the proponents' contact information for more details, comply with the Fair Political Practices Act? Is the newsletter cost a reportable contribution?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from County Counsel to FPPC, not an FPPC response letter. No CONCLUSION section exists. The letter references prior internal County Counsel advice from June 1992 but seeks external FPPC guidance. The document includes the actual newsletter article as an attachment showing the 'Measure A Initiative' content and proponents' contact information.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: On behalf of the Santa Clara County Transit District, I request advice as to whether the attached article on "Measure A," which appeared in Transit Newsletters, is affected by any of the laws within your jurisdiction. Specifically, whether the cost of the newsletter is a reportable contribution and whether the newsletter's content, which lists only proponents of Measure A with their contact information, constitutes permissible information dissemination under the Fair Political Practices Act.

embedding.summary: Santa Clara County Counsel Steven Woodside requests FPPC advice regarding whether a Transit District newsletter article on a ballot measure (Measure A) complies with campaign finance laws, particularly concerning whether the article's inclusion of only proponents' contact information (with no opposition listed) and the newsletter's cost raises reportable contribution issues.
